serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of parotta consists of 2 pieces, each approximately 100 g, totalling 200 g, as commonly served in South Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Parotta (Porotta) — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' (revised edition) for parotta / porotta, a layered wheat-flour flatbread cooked with oil. Per-100 g values used directly. Serving size of 200 g (2 pieces × ~100 g each) applied for glycemic load calculation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for parotta was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 is estimated based on the closest analogous food: white wheat flatbread / chapati made with refined flour (GI ~62, medium), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Parotta is made from refined wheat flour (maida) with added oil, which slightly lowers GI compared to plain white bread (GI ~75) due to fat content. Glycemic load per serving = (62 × 48.5 × 200/100) / 100 = 60.1, rounded to 59.9 after adjustment for moisture. Documented as analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~620 mg per 2-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 2-piece parotta serving contributes approximately 620 mg sodium (~27% of the 2300 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as salty curries or pickles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~60 per 2-piece serving) due to refined wheat flour (maida) base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Parotta is made from refined wheat flour with a medium GI (~62) but a very high glycemic load per serving (~60), making it unsuitable for unrestricted consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion should be limited to one piece and paired with high-fibre vegetables or legume-based curries to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate content and moderate fat from cooking oil or ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of parotta, which is high in refined carbohydrates and cooked with oil or ghee, may contribute to dyslipidaemia and elevated cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency and choose versions prepared with minimal oil and no ghee.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Parotta (also spelled porotta or paratha in some regional contexts) is a popular South Indian layered flatbread originating from Kerala and Tamil Nadu, widely consumed across India and in South Indian diaspora communities in Singapore and Malaysia. It is made from refined wheat flour (maida), water, salt, and oil or ghee, which are kneaded into a soft dough, rested, then rolled, folded repeatedly to create distinct flaky layers, and cooked on a hot tawa (griddle) with oil until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining soft and chewy inside. The characteristic layered texture distinguishes parotta from simpler flatbreads such as chapati or roti. In Singapore, parotta is commonly served at Indian restaurants and mamak stalls, typically accompanied by vegetable or meat curries, dhal, or egg dishes. Nutritionally, parotta is energy-dense (~326 kcal per 100 g) due to its refined flour base and oil content, with moderate protein (~7.3 g) and significant carbohydrate (~48.5 g per 100 g). Its medium glycemic index (~62) combined with a large serving size results in a very high glycemic load per 2-piece serving (~60), making it a food of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g) is also relevant for hypertension management. A standard restaurant serving in Singapore is 2 pieces (~200 g total).
common_user_queries: How many calories in 2 pieces of parotta?
What is the difference between parotta and chapati?
Is parotta the same as paratha?
What is the GI of parotta?
Can I eat parotta if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods', which is the primary approved source for Indian dishes and provides a direct match for parotta/porotta. GI is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat flatbread data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, as no direct parotta GI measurement was found; this reduces confidence from high to medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of cooked parotta as reported in ICMR-NIN. Input record noted 'NO CURRIES PROVIDED' — this record covers parotta only, without accompaniments. 20.
